paper_title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents

claims.summary: Aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation dimensions

evidence.quote: the Spearman correlation between public-leaderboard rankings and hidden-evaluation rankings was ρ=−0.13 on the execution track (n=13, statistically indistinguishable from zero) and ρ=0.69 on the planning track (n=20)
Public–hidden Spearman is ρ=0.69 for planning (n=20, robustly positive) and ρ=−0.13 for execution (n=13, p=0.71, statistically indistinguishable from zero)

claims.notes: Execution track CI spans [-0.64, +0.45], treated as suggestive only

claims.summary: Aggregate scores collapse orthogonal dimensions: different configurations with same Pass1 score exhibit qualitatively different behavior

evidence.quote: Per-rubric reasoning sensitivity. Reasoning-on vs. reasoning-off configurations score similarly on overall rubric mean but differ by 31 percentage points on clarity-specific scoring
Multi-turn artifact reuse. Plan-Execute and Supervisor-Specialist architectures score similarly on single-turn Pass1 but differ by 4.2× on turn-2-to-5 latency
Retrieval-strategy trade-off. Single-pass RAG and agentic multi-hop retrieval show 50–68% vs. ~90% accuracy with 4.5×–10× token inflation

claims.notes: Three concrete cases from independent studies on same benchmark family

claims.summary: LLM-as-Judge measurement is reflexive: leaderboard risks measuring its own judge as much as systems it evaluates

evidence.quote: Condition Insight CAR moves from 0.68 to 0.91 under a constrained-prompting design, a 20pp improvement attributable to prompting rather than to backbone-model choice
ARE/Gaia2 verifier achieves 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories
LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability reaches only Krippendorff α=0.61, well below the human–human range α∈[0.74,0.82]

claims.notes: Three independent data points from different systems

claims.summary: Out-of-distribution behavior, not in-sample mean, is the deployment question

evidence.quote: Exgentic's analysis observed cross-benchmark rank correlations of 0.32–0.85 across six heterogeneous benchmarks
current architectures do not achieve robust generalization but instead optimize for specific task distributions
leaderboard ranks reflect a user's utility function only by accident
Dehghani et al. document the 'benchmark lottery'

claims.summary: No single prior benchmark covers more than 4-5 of the 12 tiers; deployment-extension tiers (T8-T12) are absent from almost all

evidence.quote: No single prior benchmark reports more than four or five tiers; the deployment-extension tiers (T8–T12) are absent from almost all of them

claims.summary: Three OOD criteria operationalize predictive validity: held-out (mild), cross-subset (moderate), adversarial (strongest)

evidence.quote: Stratified random split of an existing benchmark... Tests whether the leaderboard ranking on a sample predicts ranking on the full population
Rank on k−1 subsets, test on the held-out subset; rotate across all subsets
Semantically-equivalent paraphrases of base scenarios across four classes: paraphrase, identifier renaming, time-window shifting, distractor injection

claims.summary: Four falsification conditions specify when the position is considered refuted

evidence.quote: Spearman rank correlation between in-sample and OOD rankings ρ<0.85 across at least two of three OOD criteria
Top-3 in-sample configurations leave top-5 on out-of-sample in ≥10% of holdout splits
Mean-vs.-OOD-variance correlation ρ_Pearson>0.2
PV-ranking top-10 differs from mean-ranking top-10 with Jaccard overlap <0.85
Patel et al. report execution-track public–private Spearman ρ=−0.13, well below the 0.85 threshold

claims.notes: Condition 1 partially supported by CODS-2025 execution track data

claims.summary: 14 parallel implementation studies converge on same measurement dimensions from distinct architectural starting points

evidence.quote: Reasoning-on raised total latency by 21.5% (15.08 s to 18.32 s) and planning latency by 41.9%
35.5% contained unnecessary calls to the expensive Deep-TSFM tool, 96% of them on wrong-domain scenarios
Replacing MCP tool execution with text-RAG collapsed remaining-useful-life pass-all-3 from 100% to 20%

claims.notes: Authors frame as convergent architectural sensitivity rather than independent triangulation

claims.summary: MCP-stdio subprocess overhead is a dominant per-call latency floor

evidence.quote: Groups G7, G9, G12, and G27 independently measure MCP-stdio subprocess overhead as a dominant per-call latency floor
G7 reports a 5–6s residual per call... G9 measures discovery as 2–3s per query

claims.summary: Judge-independent verification is both feasible and necessary

evidence.quote: ARE/Gaia2's DAG oracle does analogous trajectory-level verification, achieving 0.99 precision and 0.95 recall against 450 hand-labeled trajectories
PHMForge: LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability sits at Krippendorff α=0.61 on a 30-scenario stratified sample

claims.summary: Substrate, asset, and query-formulation are deployment-relevant axes invisible to fixed-substrate leaderboards

evidence.quote: Withholding domain tools took completion from 80.8% down to 25%
Cross-equipment transfer dropped pass rate from 84.1% on bearings to 42.7% on motors
Operator-style fuzzy queries fell from 80.6% to 48.6% (McNemar p=0.002)
